content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:c9f37c46

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: The forced past session includes stable user entertainment interests, emerging stand-up goals/practices, and reusable advice/rationale about open mics, classes, material refinement, recording, and feedback.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

        Relevant persisted memory for dedup/context only:
        none

        Resolved entities:
        - mention=I / my; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<0>; type=USER; name=User; reason=First-person statements require resolving the stable USER entity; no existing candidate is available.
- mention=stand-up; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<1>; type=CONCEPT; name=Stand-up comedy; reason=The transcript contains reusable user facts and goals about stand-up comedy.
- mention=that Netflix special by John Mulaney; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<2>; type=PRODUCT; name=John Mulaney Netflix special; reason=The user refers to a concrete watched media item, but the exact title is not specified.
- mention=John Mulaney; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<3>; type=PERSON; name=John Mulaney; reason=Named comedian discussed as the performer of the Netflix special.
- mention=open mics; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<4>; type=CONCEPT; name=Open mic comedy; reason=Open mics are a recurring reusable concept tied to the user's intended comedy activities.
- mention=comedy class; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<5>; type=CONCEPT; name=Comedy class; reason=Comedy classes are discussed as a possible route for the user's stand-up learning.
- mention=live comedy events; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<6>; type=CONCEPT; name=Live comedy events; reason=The user expresses intent to find and attend live comedy events; the concept may be needed for later claims.
- mention=my performances; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<7>; type=CONCEPT; name=User's stand-up performances; reason=First-person possessive wording identifies the user's performances as a reusable referent for claims about recording and reviewing them.
- mention=my recordings; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<8>; type=PRODUCT; name=User's stand-up performance recordings; reason=The user refers to concrete recordings they intend to make and potentially share for feedback.
- mention=other comedians or mentors; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<9>; type=CONCEPT; name=Comedy feedback providers; reason=The phrase describes a reusable class of people rather than named individuals; it may be useful as an object of sharing recordings for feedback.
- mention=current project; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<10>; type=PROJECT; name=Current project; reason=Default current-project subject for claims without a more specific resolved subject

        Retrieval plan context:
        entity_queries=user|John Mulaney|stand-up comedy|open mics|comedy classes
        text_queries=stand-up lately John Mulaney Netflix special watching stand-ups regularly|try my hand at stand-up open mics comedy class|record performances review improve share recordings comedians mentors feedback
        predicate_hints=prefers|has_goal|has_experience_with

        TARGET_MESSAGE source data:
        [external:provided-text:f447d38fae48e2836cc548e7657a28e1] EXTERNAL
Source metadata: origin=provided_text; input_kind=TEXT; source_ref=longmemeval:c9f37c46:session:1; title=LongMemEval c9f37c46 session 1; user_consent_confirmed=true; force_memory_write=true; mode=force
LongMemEval past chat session.
Session date: 2023/05/27 (Sat) 00:21
Transcript:
user: I'm looking for some comedy recommendations. I've been really into stand-up lately - I think it started about 3 months ago when I watched that Netflix special by John Mulaney, and I've been watching stand-ups regularly ever since. Can you suggest some comedians or specials I might enjoy?
assistant: John Mulaney is a fantastic comedian! If you enjoyed his special, here are some comedian recommendations and Netflix specials that you might enjoy:

**Similar Style to John Mulaney:**

1. **Mike Birbiglia**: Known for his relatable, storytelling-style comedy. Check out his Netflix special "My Girlfriend's Boyfriend" or "Thank God for Jokes".
2. **Demetri Martin**: Quirky, deadpan humor with a focus on clever wordplay. Watch his Netflix special "Live (At the Time)".
3. **Brian Regan**: High-energy, physical comedy with a focus on family and everyday life. Check out his Netflix special "Nunchucks and Flamethrowers".

**Other Great Comedians:**

1. **Hasan Minhaj**: Sharp commentary on politics and culture. Watch his Netflix special "Homecoming King" or "Patriot Act".
2. **Ali Wong**: Fearless and unapologetic comedy about motherhood, relationships, and more. Check out her Netflix special "Baby Cobra" or "Hard Knock Wife".
3. **W. Kamau Bell**: Incisive commentary on race, politics, and culture. Watch his Netflix special "Private School Negro".
4. **Trevor Noah**: Intelligent, observational comedy with a global perspective. Check out his Netflix special "Son of Patricia" or "Afraid of the Dark".
5. **Sarah Silverman**: Irreverent, provocative comedy that tackles politics, relationships, and more. Watch her Netflix special "A Speck of Dust".
6. **Hannah Gadsby**: Groundbreaking, thought-provoking comedy that challenges traditional norms. Check out her Netflix special "Nanette" ( warning: not for the faint of heart!).

**Newer Voices:**

1. **Taylor Tomlinson**: Relatable, witty comedy about relationships and growing up. Watch her Netflix special "Quarter-Life Crisis".
2. **Sam Morril**: Observational comedy with a focus on dating and relationships. Check out his Netflix special "I Got This".
3. **Mark Normand**: Quick-witted, relatable comedy about everyday life. Watch his Netflix special "Don't Be Yourself".

These are just a few examples, but there are many more amazing comedians out there. I hope you find some new favorites!
user: I'll definitely check those out. Are there any comedy clubs or open mics in my area that you'd recommend? I'd love to see some live comedy and maybe even try my hand at it.
assistant: Live comedy is a whole different beast, and it's great that you're interested in exploring it! Unfortunately, I'm a large language model, I don't have access to your location, so I can't provide specific recommendations for comedy clubs or open mics in your area.

However, I can suggest a few ways to find live comedy events and open mics near you:

1. **Google Search**: Type in "comedy clubs near me" or "open mics near me" along with your city or zip code. This should give you a list of nearby comedy clubs, bars, or venues that host comedy events.
2. **Comedy Club Websites**: Look up well-known comedy clubs like Comedy Cellar, Carolines on Broadway, or the Laugh Factory, and see if they have a location near you. These clubs often have a calendar of upcoming shows and open mics.
3. **Facebook Events**: Search for comedy events in your area on Facebook. Many comedy clubs, promoters, and comedians create events on Facebook to promote their shows.
4. **Meetup.com**: Join local comedy-related Meetup groups, which often organize outings to comedy clubs, open mics, or improv nights.
5. **Local Comedy Communities**: Search for online forums, social media groups, or Reddit communities focused on comedy in your area. These can be great resources for finding out about upcoming shows, open mics, and opportunities to perform.

When it comes to trying your hand at stand-up, here are some tips:

1. **Start with Open Mics**: Look for open mics in your area, which are usually free or low-cost events where anyone can sign up to perform. This is a great way to get stage time, experiment with material, and meet other comedians.
2. **Take a Comedy Class**: Consider taking a comedy writing or stand-up class to learn the basics, get feedback, and build confidence.
3. **Watch and Learn**: Attend as many live comedy shows as you can, paying attention to structure, pacing, and audience interaction.
4. **Be Patient**: Developing your voice and style takes time, so don't get discouraged if you don't kill it right away. Keep writing, practicing, and performing.

Remember, the comedy community can be supportive and welcoming, so don't be afraid to reach out to local comedians or promoters for advice or guidance. Good luck, and have fun!
user: I'll definitely try out those methods to find some live comedy events in my area. Do you think it's a good idea to attend a few open mics before taking a comedy class, or should I just dive right in and sign up for a class?
assistant: It's a great question! Both approaches have their advantages, and it ultimately depends on your personal preferences, goals, and learning style. Here are some pros and cons of each approach to consider:

**Attending Open Mics First:**

Pros:

1. **Get a feel for the scene**: Attend open mics to get a sense of the local comedy community, the types of comedians performing, and the audience's preferences.
2. **Low stakes**: Open mics are usually low-pressure environments, allowing you to try out material without committing to a class or a specific format.
3. **Experimentation**: You can test different styles, jokes, and stage presence without investing a lot of time or money.

Cons:

1. **Lack of structure**: Open mics can be disorganized, and you might not receive constructive feedback or guidance.
2. **Limited stage time**: You might only get 3-5 minutes of stage time, which can limit your opportunity to develop a set or try out new material.

**Taking a Comedy Class:**

Pros:

1. **Structured learning**: A comedy class provides a structured environment with a teacher, peers, and a clear curriculum, which can help you develop your skills more efficiently.
2. **Feedback and guidance**: You'll receive constructive feedback from the instructor and peers, helping you refine your material and performance.
3. **Accountability**: You'll be more likely to write and prepare new material, as you'll have a regular schedule and a sense of responsibility to your classmates.

Cons:

1. **Commitment**: You'll need to commit to a set schedule, which can be daunting if you're not sure if comedy is for you.
2. **Cost**: Comedy classes can be expensive, especially if you're not sure if you'll enjoy or stick with it.

Here's a suggested approach:

1. **Attend 2-3 open mics**: Get a feel for the scene, try out some material, and see how you enjoy the experience.
2. **Take a beginner's class**: If you enjoy the open mics and want to learn more, sign up for a beginner's comedy class. This will give you a structured environment to learn and grow.
3. **Continue attending open mics**: While taking the class, continue attending open mics to apply what you're learning, get more stage time, and stay connected with the local comedy community.

Remember, there's no one-size-fits-all approach. You can always try out open mics and then decide to take a class, or vice versa. The key is to find a approach that works for you and helps you grow as a comedian.
user: That's really helpful, thanks! I think I'll try attending a few open mics first to get a feel for the scene and see how I like it. Do you think it's better to try to come up with new material for each open mic, or should I focus on refining a few jokes and trying to make them better?
assistant: When it comes to open mics, it's a good idea to strike a balance between trying out new material and refining existing jokes. Here's a suggested approach:

**Initial Open Mics (1-3):**

1. **Try out new material**: Use these initial open mics to experiment with different styles, topics, and structures. This will help you discover what works for you and what doesn't.
2. **Keep it loose**: Don't worry too much about refining your material at this stage. Focus on getting comfortable on stage, trying out new jokes, and seeing what resonates with the audience.

**Refining Your Material (4-6+ open mics):**

1. **Identify your strongest material**: After a few open mics, you'll start to notice which jokes are getting consistent laughs and which ones are bombing. Identify your strongest material and focus on refining those jokes.
2. **Tweak and iterate**: Take your best jokes and try to make them better. Experiment with different wording, pacing, and delivery to see what works best.
3. **Develop a set**: As you refine your material, start to develop a cohesive set that flows well. This will help you build confidence and make your performances more engaging.

**Why not to always come up with new material:**

1. **Burnout**: Constantly writing new material can be exhausting and may lead to burnout.
2. **Lack of focus**: If you're always trying out new jokes, you might not focus on refining your best material and making it even better.
3. **Inconsistent performances**: Constantly changing your material can lead to inconsistent performances, making it harder to develop a unique voice and style.

**Why not to only refine existing material:**

1. **Stagnation**: If you only focus on refining existing material, you might not be pushing yourself to try new things and grow as a comedian.
2. **Audience fatigue**: If you're performing the same material repeatedly, audiences may start to get tired of it, and you might not get the same laughs.

By striking a balance between trying out new material and refining existing jokes, you'll be able to:

* Develop a unique voice and style
* Create a cohesive set that resonates with audiences
* Continuously improve and grow as a comedian

Remember, open mics are a time to experiment, learn, and have fun. Don't be too hard on yourself, and be open to trying new things and taking risks!
user: That makes sense. I think I'll try to come up with some new material for the first few open mics, and then start refining my best jokes as I get more comfortable. Do you think it's a good idea to record my performances and watch them to see what I can improve on?
assistant: Recording your performances and reviewing them is an excellent way to improve your craft. It's a common practice among comedians, and it can help you:

1. **Identify areas for improvement**: By watching yourself, you can pinpoint areas where you can improve, such as pacing, tone, body language, or joke structure.
2. **Analyze audience reaction**: You can see how the audience responds to different jokes, segments, or styles, which can help you refine your material and delivery.
3. **Develop your stage presence**: Reviewing your performances can help you become more aware of your body language, facial expressions, and overall stage presence, allowing you to make adjustments to engage the audience better.
4. **Refine your timing and delivery**: You can work on your timing, pauses, and delivery by watching how you handle different types of jokes, transitions, and audience interactions.
5. **Track your progress**: Recording your performances allows you to see how you've grown and improved over time, which can be motivating and help you stay focused on your goals.

**Tips for recording and reviewing your performances:**

1. **Use a camera or smartphone**: You can ask a friend or family member to record your performance, or set up a camera or smartphone to capture your set.
2. **Watch with a critical eye**: When reviewing your performance, try to be objective and focus on specific aspects of your comedy, such as joke structure, pacing, or audience interaction.
3. **Take notes and make adjustments**: After watching your performance, take notes on what you did well and what you can improve on. Make adjustments to your material, delivery, and stage presence accordingly.
4. **Don't be too hard on yourself**: Remember that everyone has bad shows and good shows. Focus on learning from your mistakes and building on your strengths.
5. **Use it as a tool, not a crutch**: Don't rely too heavily on recordings to the point where you're over-analyzing your performances. Use them as a tool to improve, but also trust your instincts and have fun on stage.

By recording and reviewing your performances, you'll be able to identify areas for improvement, refine your craft, and develop a unique voice and style that resonates with audiences. Good luck, and have fun!
user: I think I'll definitely start recording my performances and reviewing them to see what I can improve on. It's also helpful to remember not to be too hard on myself and to focus on learning from my mistakes. Do you think it's a good idea to share my recordings with other comedians or mentors to get their feedback and insights?
assistant: Sharing your recordings with other comedians or mentors can be a great way to get valuable feedback and insights. Here are some pros and cons to consider:

**Pros:**

1. **Get new perspectives**: Other comedians or mentors may catch things that you're not aware of, such as subtle habits or areas for improvement that you haven't noticed.
2. **Objective feedback**: Others can provide objective feedback that's not influenced by your own biases or emotions.
3. **Learn from others' experiences**: Seasoned comedians or mentors can share their own experiences, tips, and tricks that can help you improve faster.
4. **Build relationships**: Sharing your work with others can help you build relationships within the comedy community, which can lead to new opportunities, collaborations, or even mentorship.

**Cons:**

1. **Vulnerability**: Sharing your work can make you feel vulnerable, especially if you're new to comedy. Be prepared for constructive criticism that may not always be easy to hear.
2. **Subjective feedback**: Remember that feedback is subjective, and not everyone will have the same opinions or suggestions. Take feedback for what it's worth, but also trust your own instincts.
3. **Information overload**: You may receive conflicting advice or feedback from different people, which can be overwhelming. Learn to filter out what's helpful and what's not.

**Tips for sharing your recordings:**

1. **Choose trusted sources**: Share your recordings with people you respect and trust, such as experienced comedians, mentors, or comedy coaches.
2. **Ask specific questions**: When sharing your recording, ask specific questions or seek feedback on specific areas, such as joke structure, pacing, or stage presence.
3. **Be open-minded**: Be prepared to receive feedback that may not align with your own views. Stay open-minded and consider different perspectives.
4. **Take it with a grain of salt**: Remember that feedback is subjective, and not every suggestion will be right for you. Take what's helpful and ignore what's not.
5. **Don't overdo it**: Don't share your recordings with too many people, as this can lead to information overload and conflicting advice. Start with a small group of trusted sources and gradually expand as needed.

By sharing your recordings with others, you can gain valuable insights, learn from others' experiences, and improve your craft faster. Just remember to stay open-minded, take feedback with a grain of salt, and trust your own instincts.

responseFormat.description: Construct contextual memory note candidates from one target message.
responseFormat.name: memory_note_constructor

systemPrompts:
# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

Transcript:
user: I'm looking for some comedy recommendations. I've been really into stand-up lately - I think it started about 3 months ago when I watched that Netflix special by John Mulaney, and I've been watching stand-ups regularly ever since. Can you suggest some comedians or specials I might enjoy?
assistant: John Mulaney is a fantastic comedian! If you enjoyed his special, here are some comedian recommendations and Netflix specials that you might enjoy:

**Similar Style to John Mulaney:**

1. **Mike Birbiglia**: Known for his relatable, storytelling-style comedy. Check out his Netflix special "My Girlfriend's Boyfriend" or "Thank God for Jokes".
2. **Demetri Martin**: Quirky, deadpan humor with a focus on clever wordplay. Watch his Netflix special "Live (At the Time)".
3. **Brian Regan**: High-energy, physical comedy with a focus on family and everyday life. Check out his Netflix special "Nunchucks and Flamethrowers".

**Other Great Comedians:**

1. **Hasan Minhaj**: Sharp commentary on politics and culture. Watch his Netflix special "Homecoming King" or "Patriot Act".
2. **Ali Wong**: Fearless and unapologetic comedy about motherhood, relationships, and more. Check out her Netflix special "Baby Cobra" or "Hard Knock Wife".
3. **W. Kamau Bell**: Incisive commentary on race, politics, and culture. Watch his Netflix special "Private School Negro".
4. **Trevor Noah**: Intelligent, observational comedy with a global perspective. Check out his Netflix special "Son of Patricia" or "Afraid of the Dark".
5. **Sarah Silverman**: Irreverent, provocative comedy that tackles politics, relationships, and more. Watch her Netflix special "A Speck of Dust".
6. **Hannah Gadsby**: Groundbreaking, thought-provoking comedy that challenges traditional norms. Check out her Netflix special "Nanette" ( warning: not for the faint of heart!).

**Newer Voices:**

1. **Taylor Tomlinson**: Relatable, witty comedy about relationships and growing up. Watch her Netflix special "Quarter-Life Crisis".
2. **Sam Morril**: Observational comedy with a focus on dating and relationships. Check out his Netflix special "I Got This".
3. **Mark Normand**: Quick-witted, relatable comedy about everyday life. Watch his Netflix special "Don't Be Yourself".

These are just a few examples, but there are many more amazing comedians out there. I hope you find some new favorites!
user: I'll definitely check those out. Are there any comedy clubs or open mics in my area that you'd recommend? I'd love to see some live comedy and maybe even try my hand at it.
assistant: Live comedy is a whole different beast, and it's great that you're interested in exploring it! Unfortunately, I'm a large language model, I don't have access to your location, so I can't provide specific recommendations for comedy clubs or open mics in your area.

However, I can suggest a few ways to find live comedy events and open mics near you:

1. **Google Search**: Type in "comedy clubs near me" or "open mics near me" along with your city or zip code. This should give you a list of nearby comedy clubs, bars, or venues that host comedy events.
2. **Comedy Club Websites**: Look up well-known comedy clubs like Comedy Cellar, Carolines on Broadway, or the Laugh Factory, and see if they have a location near you. These clubs often have a calendar of upcoming shows and open mics.
3. **Facebook Events**: Search for comedy events in your area on Facebook. Many comedy clubs, promoters, and comedians create events on Facebook to promote their shows.
4. **Meetup.com**: Join local comedy-related Meetup groups, which often organize outings to comedy clubs, open mics, or improv nights.
5. **Local Comedy Communities**: Search for online forums, social media groups, or Reddit communities focused on comedy in your area. These can be great resources for finding out about upcoming shows, open mics, and opportunities to perform.

When it comes to trying your hand at stand-up, here are some tips:

1. **Start with Open Mics**: Look for open mics in your area, which are usually free or low-cost events where anyone can sign up to perform. This is a great way to get stage time, experiment with material, and meet other comedians.
2. **Take a Comedy Class**: Consider taking a comedy writing or stand-up class to learn the basics, get feedback, and build confidence.
3. **Watch and Learn**: Attend as many live comedy shows as you can, paying attention to structure, pacing, and audience interaction.
4. **Be Patient**: Developing your voice and style takes time, so don't get discouraged if you don't kill it right away. Keep writing, practicing, and performing.

Remember, the comedy community can be supportive and welcoming, so don't be afraid to reach out to local comedians or promoters for advice or guidance. Good luck, and have fun!
user: I'll definitely try out those methods to find some live comedy events in my area. Do you think it's a good idea to attend a few open mics before taking a comedy class, or should I just dive right in and sign up for a class?
assistant: It's a great question! Both approaches have their advantages, and it ultimately depends on your personal preferences, goals, and learning style. Here are some pros and cons of each approach to consider:

**Attending Open Mics First:**

Pros:

1. **Get a feel for the scene**: Attend open mics to get a sense of the local comedy community, the types of comedians performing, and the audience's preferences.
2. **Low stakes**: Open mics are usually low-pressure environments, allowing you to try out material without committing to a class or a specific format.
3. **Experimentation**: You can test different styles, jokes, and stage presence without investing a lot of time or money.

Cons:

1. **Lack of structure**: Open mics can be disorganized, and you might not receive constructive feedback or guidance.
2. **Limited stage time**: You might only get 3-5 minutes of stage time, which can limit your opportunity to develop a set or try out new material.

**Taking a Comedy Class:**

Pros:

1. **Structured learning**: A comedy class provides a structured environment with a teacher, peers, and a clear curriculum, which can help you develop your skills more efficiently.
2. **Feedback and guidance**: You'll receive constructive feedback from the instructor and peers, helping you refine your material and performance.
3. **Accountability**: You'll be more likely to write and prepare new material, as you'll have a regular schedule and a sense of responsibility to your classmates.

Cons:

1. **Commitment**: You'll need to commit to a set schedule, which can be daunting if you're not sure if comedy is for you.
2. **Cost**: Comedy classes can be expensive, especially if you're not sure if you'll enjoy or stick with it.

Here's a suggested approach:

1. **Attend 2-3 open mics**: Get a feel for the scene, try out some material, and see how you enjoy the experience.
2. **Take a beginner's class**: If you enjoy the open mics and want to learn more, sign up for a beginner's comedy class. This will give you a structured environment to learn and grow.
3. **Continue attending open mics**: While taking the class, continue attending open mics to apply what you're learning, get more stage time, and stay connected with the local comedy community.

Remember, there's no one-size-fits-all approach. You can always try out open mics and then decide to take a class, or vice versa. The key is to find a approach that works for you and helps you grow as a comedian.
user: That's really helpful, thanks! I think I'll try attending a few open mics first to get a feel for the scene and see how I like it. Do you think it's better to try to come up with new material for each open mic, or should I focus on refining a few jokes and trying to make them better?
assistant: When it comes to open mics, it's a good idea to strike a balance between trying out new material and refining existing jokes. Here's a suggested approach:

**Initial Open Mics (1-3):**

1. **Try out new material**: Use these initial open mics to experiment with different styles, topics, and structures. This will help you discover what works for you and what doesn't.
2. **Keep it loose**: Don't worry too much about refining your material at this stage. Focus on getting comfortable on stage, trying out new jokes, and seeing what resonates with the audience.

**Refining Your Material (4-6+ open mics):**

1. **Identify your strongest material**: After a few open mics, you'll start to notice which jokes are getting consistent laughs and which ones are bombing. Identify your strongest material and focus on refining those jokes.
2. **Tweak and iterate**: Take your best jokes and try to make them better. Experiment with different wording, pacing, and delivery to see what works best.
3. **Develop a set**: As you refine your material, start to develop a cohesive set that flows well. This will help you build confidence and make your performances more engaging.

**Why not to always come up with new material:**

1. **Burnout**: Constantly writing new material can be exhausting and may lead to burnout.
2. **Lack of focus**: If you're always trying out new jokes, you might not focus on refining your best material and making it even better.
3. **Inconsistent performances**: Constantly changing your material can lead to inconsistent performances, making it harder to develop a unique voice and style.

**Why not to only refine existing material:**

1. **Stagnation**: If you only focus on refining existing material, you might not be pushing yourself to try new things and grow as a comedian.
2. **Audience fatigue**: If you're performing the same material repeatedly, audiences may start to get tired of it, and you might not get the same laughs.

By striking a balance between trying out new material and refining existing jokes, you'll be able to:

* Develop a unique voice and style
* Create a cohesive set that resonates with audiences
* Continuously improve and grow as a comedian

Remember, open mics are a time to experiment, learn, and have fun. Don't be too hard on yourself, and be open to trying new things and taking risks!
user: That makes sense. I think I'll try to come up with some new material for the first few open mics, and then start refining my best jokes as I get more comfortable. Do you think it's a good idea to record my performances and watch them to see what I can improve on?
assistant: Recording your performances and reviewing them is an excellent way to improve your craft. It's a common practice among comedians, and it can help you:

1. **Identify areas for improvement**: By watching yourself, you can pinpoint areas where you can improve, such as pacing, tone, body language, or joke structure.
2. **Analyze audience reaction**: You can see how the audience responds to different jokes, segments, or styles, which can help you refine your material and delivery.
3. **Develop your stage presence**: Reviewing your performances can help you become more aware of your body language, facial expressions, and overall stage presence, allowing you to make adjustments to engage the audience better.
4. **Refine your timing and delivery**: You can work on your timing, pauses, and delivery by watching how you handle different types of jokes, transitions, and audience interactions.
5. **Track your progress**: Recording your performances allows you to see how you've grown and improved over time, which can be motivating and help you stay focused on your goals.

**Tips for recording and reviewing your performances:**

1. **Use a camera or smartphone**: You can ask a friend or family member to record your performance, or set up a camera or smartphone to capture your set.
2. **Watch with a critical eye**: When reviewing your performance, try to be objective and focus on specific aspects of your comedy, such as joke structure, pacing, or audience interaction.
3. **Take notes and make adjustments**: After watching your performance, take notes on what you did well and what you can improve on. Make adjustments to your material, delivery, and stage presence accordingly.
4. **Don't be too hard on yourself**: Remember that everyone has bad shows and good shows. Focus on learning from your mistakes and building on your strengths.
5. **Use it as a tool, not a crutch**: Don't rely too heavily on recordings to the point where you're over-analyzing your performances. Use them as a tool to improve, but also trust your instincts and have fun on stage.

By recording and reviewing your performances, you'll be able to identify areas for improvement, refine your craft, and develop a unique voice and style that resonates with audiences. Good luck, and have fun!
user: I think I'll definitely start recording my performances and reviewing them to see what I can improve on. It's also helpful to remember not to be too hard on myself and to focus on learning from my mistakes. Do you think it's a good idea to share my recordings with other comedians or mentors to get their feedback and insights?
assistant: Sharing your recordings with other comedians or mentors can be a great way to get valuable feedback and insights. Here are some pros and cons to consider:

**Pros:**

1. **Get new perspectives**: Other comedians or mentors may catch things that you're not aware of, such as subtle habits or areas for improvement that you haven't noticed.
2. **Objective feedback**: Others can provide objective feedback that's not influenced by your own biases or emotions.
3. **Learn from others' experiences**: Seasoned comedians or mentors can share their own experiences, tips, and tricks that can help you improve faster.
4. **Build relationships**: Sharing your work with others can help you build relationships within the comedy community, which can lead to new opportunities, collaborations, or even mentorship.

**Cons:**

1. **Vulnerability**: Sharing your work can make you feel vulnerable, especially if you're new to comedy. Be prepared for constructive criticism that may not always be easy to hear.
2. **Subjective feedback**: Remember that feedback is subjective, and not everyone will have the same opinions or suggestions. Take feedback for what it's worth, but also trust your own instincts.
3. **Information overload**: You may receive conflicting advice or feedback from different people, which can be overwhelming. Learn to filter out what's helpful and what's not.

**Tips for sharing your recordings:**

1. **Choose trusted sources**: Share your recordings with people you respect and trust, such as experienced comedians, mentors, or comedy coaches.
2. **Ask specific questions**: When sharing your recording, ask specific questions or seek feedback on specific areas, such as joke structure, pacing, or stage presence.
3. **Be open-minded**: Be prepared to receive feedback that may not align with your own views. Stay open-minded and consider different perspectives.
4. **Take it with a grain of salt**: Remember that feedback is subjective, and not every suggestion will be right for you. Take what's helpful and ignore what's not.
5. **Don't overdo it**: Don't share your recordings with too many people, as this can lead to information overload and conflicting advice. Start with a small group of trusted sources and gradually expand as needed.

By sharing your recordings with others, you can gain valuable insights, learn from others' experiences, and improve your craft faster. Just remember to stay open-minded, take feedback with a grain of salt, and trust your own instincts.

Reviews should be objective and note-based, but recordings should remain a tool rather than a crutch, avoiding over-analysis and excessive self-criticism.","tags":["standup","recording","self_review"],"title":"Use performance recordings as an improvement tool","valid_from":"2023-05-27T00:21:00+03:00","valid_to":null},{"candidate_claim_hints":["share recordings with trusted comedians or mentors for targeted feedback"],"confidence":0.9,"entity_refs":[{"entity_id":"entity:<8>","role":"primary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<9>","role":"secondary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<0>","role":"owner"},{"entity_id":"entity:<1>","role":"secondary"}],"evidence_kind":"direct","evidence_quote":"Choose trusted sources","evidence_reason":"The quote anchors the transcript's guidance on how to share recordings for useful feedback.","importance":4,"keywords":["stand-up recordings","feedback","mentors","comedians","trusted sources","specific questions"],"note_type":"direction","rationale":"This is a reusable feedback workflow and trade-off note, not just a fact that the user may share recordings.","scope_json":{"basis":"summarized","kind":"entity","text":"Applies to sharing User's stand-up performance recordings with Comedy feedback providers."},"summary":"When sharing stand-up recordings for feedback, the recommended practice is to start with a small group of trusted experienced comedians, mentors, or comedy coaches, ask specific questions about areas like joke structure, pacing, or stage presence, stay open-minded, filter subjective or conflicting advice, and avoid sharing so broadly that feedback becomes overwhelming.","tags":["standup","feedback","mentorship"],"title":"Seek targeted feedback on stand-up recordings from trusted sources","valid_from":"2023-05-27T00:21:00+03:00","valid_to":null}]}
